text:
Figure 7:


In the given diagram, a light ray is passing through a lens. To complete the path of the light ray through the section of the lens, we need to consider the refraction of light at both faces of the lens.


At the first face of the lens (air-lens interface), the light ray is refracted towards the normal line. This is because light is passing from a rarer medium (air) to a denser medium (lens). The angle of incidence is greater than the angle of refraction.


To draw the refracted ray at the first face, we need to draw the normal line at the point of incidence. The normal line is perpendicular to the surface of the lens. The refracted ray lies between the incident ray and the normal line.


At the second face of the lens (lens-air interface), the light ray is refracted away from the normal line. This is because light is passing from a denser medium (lens) to a rarer medium (air). The angle of incidence is less than the angle of refraction.


To draw the refracted ray at the second face, we need to draw the normal line at the point of incidence. The normal line is perpendicular to the surface of the lens. The refracted ray lies away from the incident ray and the normal line.


The completed path of the light ray through the section of the lens is shown in Figure 7. The light ray is refracted towards the normal line at the first face and away from the normal line at the second face.


Key points:


- Refraction occurs at both faces of the lens.
- The normal line is perpendicular to the surface of the lens at each face.
- The angle of incidence is greater than the angle of refraction at the first face (air-lens interface).
- The angle of incidence is less than the angle of refraction at the second face (lens-air interface).
- The refracted ray lies between the incident ray and the normal line at the first face.
- The refracted ray lies away from the incident ray and the normal line at the second face.
